[114:20]we are going to be talking about news tonight. We're going to be getting into a couple of

[115:03]the really big story is that this is the eight year

[115:07]anniversary of the show, February 6th, 2025.

[115:14]It has been eight years to the day

[115:17]since this show started back in 2017.

[115:22]pretty big milestone. And I got to tell you, all it does is make me feel old. I feel pretty

[119:08]period and I had been creating content around that time when I was in high school and

[119:33]we were feeling that optimism,

[119:37]that sense of total victory, smugness, gloating,

[120:43]But when Trump won the election in November and during that transition period, I thought

[120:51]because there was the four years under Trump, which I covered nearly every single day of it.

[120:57]I started the show two weeks after the inauguration. So I was here every day for the whole thing.

[121:04]And then I was very involved in Stop the Steel. I was at January 6th.

[121:09]And then I covered every single day of the Biden administration. And now we're back here again

[121:16]when Trump is back in office.

[121:19]And categorizing it that way,

[121:21]it's pretty easy to think about the attitude of the show.

[121:25]The first four years were very optimistic

[121:28]and very sunny and cheerful and positive

[121:32]because Trump had won.

[121:34]It seemed like anything was possible.

[121:36]It was only later that we were disappointed

[121:39]and the crushing political reality set in.

[121:43]Some of the mistakes, betrayals,

[121:46]other issues in the first administration.

[121:49]And then the second period, the Biden administration

[121:52]was, of course, unambiguously brutal.

[121:56]It was extremely dark, miserable.

[122:00]We were persecuted more than ever before, censored,

[122:04]attacked by the government, among others.

[122:07]There was intense division inside the movement,

[122:11]all of the pressure from the government

[122:12]and from big tech and other forces.

[122:16]I think created a lot of resentment and animosity

[122:19]between me and friends and other people

[122:22]that were forced underground.

[122:24]And so when you think about those two periods,

[122:28]they were very distinct.

[122:30]And it was very clear what the mood was.

[122:35]Four years of Trump were optimistic and fun and light

[122:40]because we were in power.

[122:42]We also had not yet seen what Trump governing looked like,

[122:46]so there was this innocence that we had.

[122:49]Four years under Biden was the complete opposite.

[122:53]If the first four years was the morning in America,

[122:57]the second four years was the dark winter.

[123:00]And we saw the reality of what it means to be

[123:03]a dissident political activist.

[123:06]The first four years, it was kind of a joke.

[123:09]It was a troll.

[123:11]We were all young and just having fun and making jokes on the internet with our friends.

[123:16]And then when Biden took power, we realized what we had actually done, what Trumpism represented.

[123:23]And on January 6th, the challenge and the threat that it posed to the system.

[123:29]It's one thing.

[123:31]to attend a Trump rally and laugh and have a good time.

[123:34]It's another thing when the seat of the American government

[123:37]during the presidential transition,

[123:39]which is the core of the constitutional system or process,

[123:44]is under siege by Trump supporters.

[123:47]It was just a different level of seriousness.

[123:50]And then for four years, it was subpoenas, no fly list, frozen money, FBI investigation,

[123:57]brutal censorship, debanking, things we didn't even think were possible or maybe that we

[124:03]thought were improbable.

[124:05]And now I guess the trick is I don't really know what to expect.

[124:16]It would follow from that pattern that this four years are going to be great because Trump is an office again

[124:22]and he won and everything is supposed to be better and

[124:26]I think that in some ways that's true

[124:28]in some ways there is a

[124:30]profound sense of relief and

[124:33]in some ways things have gotten materially easier and better for me

[124:38]with Elon's acquisition of X

[124:41]Rumble, which has been invented in the past few years,

[124:44]I should say, introduction of live streaming on Rumble,

[124:48]the rise of Bitcoin,

[124:50]maybe the prospect that debanking

[124:52]and other forms of censorship might be rolled back

[124:54]or relaxed.

[124:57]So things are certainly better.

[124:59]And yet things are stranger and more different.

[125:04]Trump is back in office, but it's not quite the same.

[125:06]We're all back on Twitter, but it's not really the same.

[125:10]It seems that our talking points are more popular than ever.

[125:15]Everybody's aware of them,

[125:16]and even normal people are talking about them.

[125:19]And yet something doesn't feel quite right.

[125:22]It almost feels like it's too good to be true.

[125:25]And that is the kind of confusion,

[125:28]I think that the dissident right is feeling at the moment,

[125:32]at least I am, and I think the groipers are.

[125:35]It's a timeline where Trump won,

[125:38]And yet it seems that the essence of his victory does not belong to spiritually the America

[126:21]globalism, globalization, versus the billionaire elite oligarchs. Now, though

[126:28]it seems like there's been some sort of synthesis, there's been a fusion of those

[129:27]or maybe no idea about me.

[129:29]And so I wanted to talk a little bit about the show

[129:32]and kind of what I'm feeling eight years in.

[129:35]And there's a point to all of this, I promise.

[129:39]It felt like for the past eight years,

[129:42]literally up until this very moment,

[129:44]or maybe up until just a very recent past,

[129:47]it feels like

[129:49]Everything that I was doing and everything that this show was,

[129:54]was in reaction to or part of a tit for tat against the system.

[130:03]And I'll explain what I mean by that.

[130:06]Eight years ago, I was a freshman in college.

[130:11]I was an unknown quantity.

[130:13]I didn't come from any kind of political background, and that's unique because what I've learned

[130:19]over the course of doing this and what maybe you people don't know is that almost everybody

[130:24]in politics, influencers, political people.

[130:30]They all have some kind of pedigree. They all have some kind of academic pedigree

[130:34]or political pedigree. Their parents are professors at some research school.

[130:40]Their parents worked in the Bush administration or the Nixon administration or something.

[130:45]Almost everybody that you see in politics is not like you, not like me. I didn't know that.

[130:51]I thought everybody was like me. You know, I grew up in the suburbs of Chicago. My parents were working class.

[130:57]They didn't even go to college.

[131:00]And when I got into this,

[131:02]I thought everybody was just a normal average person,

[131:06]but that's not really the case.

[131:08]And anyway, so I was just a regular guy.

[131:11]I mean, I was a pretty eccentric guy,

[131:13]but I had a regular background.

[131:16]And I went to school in Boston University.

[131:19]And I, like many other young people,

[131:21]like many of you guys at that time,

[131:23]and it's so funny because I see it now,

[131:25]I'm on the other side of it.

[131:27]Back in 2016 during the election. I was just a very small shit poster on Twitter and I wasn't even that red-pilled

[131:36]I wasn't even that based if I'm being honest. I was a Trump supporter

[131:41]I was a nationalist and I had been for most of my teenage years a libertarian

[131:48]basic bitch conservative

[131:50]But in 2016 I started to come around to a little bit more of a red-pilled

[131:55]reactionary politics

[131:57]And so I was a regular shit poster online.

[131:59]I was posting controversial stuff, provocative stuff as a teenager, making no money, having

[132:07]no background in this.

[132:09]And when I got onto the campus at Boston University, I was known in the campus for wearing a MAGA

[132:15]hat, for wearing a Trump hat.

[132:17]And I got on campus in September 2016 as a freshman.

[132:23]and I was wearing my MAGA hat everywhere

[132:26]and I became known as that guy.

[132:28]So a lot of young guys now,

[132:30]they're all doing that in this day and age,

[132:32]they're now doing that in 2024.

[132:35]They're the rabble rouser on the campus.

[132:39]The Trump supporter, the MAGA guy,

[132:42]they're doing a man on the street thing on TikTok.

[132:45]They're trying to trigger or troll the liberals.

[132:49]I was doing that back then.

[132:51]And I became known as that MAGA guy

[132:53]in a very liberal city, very liberal campus.

[132:57]And they asked me, some group from the student newspaper

[133:02]asked me to participate in a video

[133:04]where they were interviewing students

[133:06]about who they would vote for.

[133:08]And I was the only Trump supporter they could find.

[133:11]So I've been on campus for one month

[133:13]and I get asked by the BU Daily Paper.

[133:17]if I wanted to be a part of this piece.

[133:19]And I was interviewed with like seven Hillary Clinton supporters

[133:23]and they put me on the front page with my MAGA hat.

[133:26]And I got a lot of attention on the campus.

[133:28]People were criticizing me, attacking me.

[133:31]And I was putting out a lot of basic stuff

[133:35]saying multiculturalism as cancer or feminism sucks.

[133:38]That kind of stuff.

[133:40]And eventually I started getting threats.

[133:42]People were threatening to come and beat me up,

[133:45]knock my MAGA hat off.

[133:47]People were saying they were gonna kill me

[133:49]if they saw me on campus.

[133:50]Again, not so different than what's been happening

[133:52]lately around here.

[133:56]And so I pushed back, you know, I didn't back down.

[134:00]I didn't take down my Twitter account.

[134:02]And eventually after about a week

[134:04]of social media controversy,

[134:08]I was approached by this guy from Young Americans for Liberty,

[134:12]which is a campus organization.

[134:14]And he asked me if I'd like to debate somebody

[134:18]that was attacking me,

[134:20]if I would like to debate one of my adversaries on Twitter.

[134:25]And I was looking to make a name for myself.

[134:27]I said, yeah, absolutely.

[134:28]I was a public speaker.

[134:29]I was into that kind of thing.

[134:30]I said, yeah, let's do it.

[134:31]And so this guy went around, he asked a lot of the people

[134:34]that were shitting on me on Twitter,

[134:36]if they would face me in a debate.

[134:38]And nobody agreed to it.

[134:40]He came back to me and he said, yeah, sorry,

[134:42]no one's, no one's gonna do it.

[134:44]And I said, are you sure?

[134:46]Can you, can you just ask around one more time?

[134:49]And he did and he found one person to agree.

[134:52]And it happened to be the student body president

[134:55]of Boston University, this guy named Jacob Brewer.

[134:58]And he was the head of the student government.

[135:01]So, they set up this event, they took out space, this auditorium in one of the buildings

[135:08]there on Commonwealth Avenue.

[135:11]It was hosted by Young Americans for Liberty.

[135:13]and they advertised it in the school paper

[135:16]and about 300 people showed up, 300 students

[135:19]and they were all there to boo me.

[135:21]They were all there to heckle me and give me a hard time

[135:25]but wound up being a pretty big deal.

[135:26]So I go and I debate this senior,

[135:29]the student body president.

[135:30]We debate about Trump and Hillary

[135:33]and I take questions from the audience and it was intense.

[135:38]Feelings were very high at that time.

[135:40]This was the peak of SJWs and triggered liberal compilations and things like that.

[135:47]After the debate was over, I thought I did a pretty good job.

[135:50]This girl named Cassie Dillon jumped up on stage.

[135:55]She had been periscoping the whole thing.

[135:58]That means she was live streaming it on Twitter.

[136:02]So she had been live streaming the whole debate on Twitter and it was getting like 30,000

[136:07]viewers.

[136:09]So she jumps up on stage and says, that was unbelievable.

[136:13]That was the most unbelievable thing I've ever seen.

[136:16]You did great.

[136:17]She goes, 30,000 people were watching.

[136:20]She said, Ben Shapiro was watching.

[136:22]She said, Milo was watching.

[136:24]She said, I think you have about four job offers.

[136:27]And I was like, wow, this was my wildest dreams.

[136:32]I said, finally, a young, I had just gotten on campus.

[136:36]I was 18, super conservative, outspoken.

[136:40]I said, this is my big break.

[136:42]I can't believe it was sort of that simple.

[136:45]And so she does a little post-game interview with me

[136:49]on Periscope, which is lost to history.

[136:52]I hope someone has it.

[136:53]I don't think anyone's been able to recover the footage.

[136:57]But she does a post debate interview with me on Periscope and she asked me how do you think the debate went out?

[137:03]What do you think who do you think's gonna win the election?

[137:07]Last question she asks

[137:10]She says would you ever take a trip to Israel?

[137:15]I'm not making this up

[137:17]This is how it starts

[137:19]She goes would you take a trip to Israel? I just got back from Israel myself, and I thought it was wonderful

[137:25]It was beautiful. I had a great time

[137:28]And I said well

[137:30]Maybe I said but I think I have everything I need right here in America

[137:34]That was my answer and she goes. Oh, okay. Well good answer. And so we wrap it up

[137:39]That's how I met Cassie Dillon

[137:41]And for those that don't know, Cassie Dillon at that time was a fellow at the Daily Wire.

[137:47]She was working for Ben Shapiro.

[137:49]And she became a good friend of mine.

[137:52]I think she probably had a crush on me, but she also wanted to promote me.

[137:56]She texted Ben Shapiro that night and said, this guy is incredible.

[138:01]You have to take a look.

[138:06]She goes we can work on him and you got to take him under your wing and Shapiro goes I'll take a look

[138:13]I have the screenshots and

[138:15]So this is how we get to know each other at that time Cassie Dylan. She was working for the daily wire as a writer

[138:24]But she was also working for RSBM right side broadcasting network

[138:30]And at that time, RSBN was transitioning from covering the rallies during the election.

[138:36]After the election, they started to create original programming.

[138:41]Trump rallies were over.

[138:43]They needed a new business model.

[138:45]They couldn't make money from streaming the rallies.

[138:48]So they started to bring on opinion hosts commentary.

[138:53]and they were going to host all of these shows.

[138:57]And so they brought on Mike Cernovich,

[138:59]they brought on Bill Mitchell, Wayne Dupree,

[139:03]among others to do a show.

[139:05]And Cassie Dillon was a good friend of Joe

[139:08]and Jacob Seals who ran RSBN and they gave her a show.

[139:12]It was called Raised Right.

[139:13]and she did this show with another guy named Will Nardi,

[139:17]who was local.

[139:18]He was in Boston too.

[139:19]And so one night she asked me

[139:21]if I wanted to co-host the show and I did.

[139:25]I co-hosted the show.

[139:26]She thought I did a great job.

[139:28]And I think I did a couple of other live streams with her.

[139:31]We hung around a little bit

[139:33]and it was a little bit after that around Christmas time

[139:36]when she asked me if I would like my own show on RSBN.

[139:41]This was the beginning of America First.

[139:44]And so she pitched me and she said,

[139:46]I think I'm going to go to Joe Seals, the head of RSBN.

[139:50]And I'm going to ask him to give you a show.

[139:52]And I said, this is it.

[139:53]This is my big break.

[139:54]This is my foot in the door.

[139:57]This is how I'm going to start to build a name

[139:59]for myself in a profile.

[140:01]And at that time, I had a very small Twitter account.

[140:03]I think I had two or 3000 followers, very, very minor.

[140:08]I had been around. I knew some guys like Beardson and Paultown and usual suspects.

[140:16]Or maybe I hadn't met them yet, but I think I was starting to

[140:20]see their content and get to know them a little bit. I knew Comrade Stump. I knew a lot of those guys.

[140:27]And so I thought, this is it. This is my moment. And so it was during Trump's inauguration,

[140:32]January 20th, 2017. It's when he said, Americanism, not globalism will be our credo. It's going

[140:40]to be only America first. That's when I got the name for this show. I got the name for

[140:53]The seals brothers came to me. They said we need a name for the show

[140:56]I said, let's call it Nicholas J went to show. They said, well, nobody knows who you are. That's too long anyway

[141:03]Come up with something else

[141:05]So I watched the inauguration. I said, how about America first? I said, okay, and

[141:10]That's how it started. So they sent me a Logitech webcam. They sent me a blue Yeti snowball microphone

[141:16]They sent me some green screen set from Amazon

[141:20]And at that time I had a roommate in my freshman dorm room.

[141:25]So I asked a buddy of mine who was a Trump supporter

[141:28]and had a single.

[141:29]I said, hey, can I do my show in your room?

[141:32]And so I set up all my equipment in his room,

[141:35]my laptop, microphone, all that stuff.

[141:38]And I started the show on Right Side Broadcasting Network.

[141:42]And the rest should have been history, but this is a very important theme.

[141:48]So understand at this stage in the game, what I'm describing to you, everything up until

[141:53]that point, this is what happens to every young conservative.

[141:59]on a campus.

[142:01]This is what happens to every perspective,

[142:03]young conservative personality, influencer, political person.

[142:13]although there's some girls and there's some non-white men,

[142:16]but for the most part, if you are one of these

[142:18]young white teenagers, young white teenage guy,

[142:23]who is pretty political or conservative in high school,

[142:29]Do one of these nicky-knack, you know, young conservative political commentary deals, you

[142:35]go to a campus and this is what it looks like. Got a small following. You try and get in

[142:41]touch with one of the campus orgs, like a turning point, a YAL, a YAF, Young Americans

[142:47]for Liberty, Young Americans for Freedom group. You try to go to an event or host an event.

[142:57]something with a bigger influencer.

[142:59]And you hope that you're going to get noticed

[143:02]and recognized and amplified.

[143:04]And maybe you just might land a contract

[143:07]or a guest appearance or show up on Fox News

[143:10]or something like that.

[143:11]You hope that you might get a mentor

[143:13]or some sort of benefactor.

[143:15]This is what it looks like.

[143:17]But for me, there was one wrinkle.

[143:21]They were writing articles about me in Daily Wire

[143:25]and in Breitbart and in other conservative publications.

[143:32]and they wanted me to write for Daily Wire at one point.

[143:48]I started at this point in time after Cassie Dillon asked me that question and based on

[143:55]some of what was happening on the internet at the time, to question the conservative

[144:00]orthodoxy specifically surrounding Israel.

[144:04]And so I began to notice that Daily Wire, Prager University, Breitbart, they were all

[144:15]I couldn't help but notice.

[144:17]I would go on PragerU and they would have a video about the constitution, a video about

[144:22]low taxes, a video about the 10 commandments, something like that.

[144:29]And then there'd be a video why Gaza should be destroyed.

[144:34]And I'd say, okay, something here is not quite right.

[144:37]And then I'd keep going, why America needs a big military.

[144:41]Here's why we should get rid of the Federal Reserve.

[144:45]Women should have traditional gender roles.

[144:48]And then another one.

[144:49]Why Israel's right to exist must never be questioned.

[144:55]And so I started to question, why is it that this is part of the,

[145:01]why is this part of the assortment of talking points

[145:04]that we always get?

[145:06]Why is it seemingly all of these traditional conservative

[145:10]American talking points and ideas?

[145:14]And then this one, which is not, it's basic pattern recognition, things that are like

[145:21]versus things that are not like.

[145:23]I said, okay, low taxes, pro-second amendment, pro-first amendment, anti-feminism against

[145:30]illegal immigration, supporting Israel.

[145:33]I said, this one doesn't really square, and yet that is always present.

[145:37]And then I started to notice, well, Prager, Jewish, Shapiro, Jewish.

[145:44]I asked Elliott Hamilton, Aaron Bandler.

[145:47]These were other writers of Daily Wire.

[145:50]I said, why is it that we give 3.6 at that time? It was six. I said, why is it that we

[145:56]give $3.6 billion per year to Israel? Why are they the number one recipient of foreign

[146:01]aid? Why is there a preoccupation with Israel here? Isn't that contradictory with America

[146:06]first? That's the name of my show. After all, that's Trump's credo. He just won the election.

[146:17]I said, should not everything be consistent and aligned with America first?

[146:27]And at first they were nice, but then they were not nice about it.

[146:31]At first they said, oh, stop.

[146:34]Oh my goodness.

[146:36]Well, the reason why is because, you know, they would try to argue it, but I was persistent.

[146:45]The way that you're asking this is anti-Semitic.

[146:48]They said, you know, this is a conversation we can have.

[146:51]They said, we're willing to have this conversation with you.

[146:54]They said, but it's the way that you're talking about it.

[146:57]It's anti-Semitic and it's going to a bad place

[147:18]it specifically says to do this.

[147:50]how to handle it.

[148:06]I kept pushing on Twitter.

[148:12]refused to talk to me, Cassie Dillon sent me a long text

[149:08]what's gotten into her.

[149:10]And then in April, 2017, for the first time, a hit piece was written about me.

[149:24]for the six or seven Muslim majority countries.

[149:27]This was during the reign of ISIS and Iraq and Syria.

[149:32]And at the time, liberals and federal judges

[149:35]opposed the travel ban and tried to block it.

[149:38]They said that it was a violation of the First Amendment.

[149:47]You don't have a First Amendment right in Iraq to immigrate to the United States.

[149:53]I said, and by the way, the First Amendment properly understood, when it talks about freedom

[149:58]of religion, it was really about freedom of Christian denominations.

[150:05]It was not written for radical Muslims who their theology was to murder us.

[150:18]Among other things, there was another clip in there where I said that globalist should

[150:21]be killed or something.

[150:23]She clipped that and sent it to media matters.

[150:26]And that was the first ever hit piece written about me.

[150:29]And that's how I started to get a bad reputation.

[151:40]And so they brought me back on the network in June. I think it was actually May 29th

[151:46]It was JFK's birthday

[151:48]So they brought me back in the summer. I was back at home in Chicago and I did the show three days a week

[151:55]Then I went to a job training at the leadership Institute

[153:02]and I didn't like how I was being treated.

[153:21]and you stay there overnight.

[153:23]It's like a two week training

[153:25]and you stay in their building.

[153:31]shaking hands, everybody's nice.

[153:33]It's all cool.

[153:34]Very quickly I find out

[153:35]I'm the most conservative person there.

[153:42]why they're a conservative.

[153:44]And people raise their hand and say, well, I'm a conservative because like, I like the

[153:58]because I think that our country is changing.

[154:38]in a two week job training
